entries.claude_text: This survivor was placed on the vessel by a corporate entity. They have a specific information objective: recovering, destroying, or concealing something on this ship. They are not a trained operative — a logistics manager, a technician, a mid-level bureaucrat told this would be simple. They are frightened and increasingly aware that it will not be.

interfaces.note: This survivor knew before boarding. Their fear is guilt as much as danger.
They may have a relationship with the threat, intended or not.

entries.claude_text: The vessel's medical officer, trained in zero-g trauma care and pharmaceutical management. They have seen what the threat does to a body and cannot stop cataloguing symptoms. They are professionally calm but privately terrified, and their medical supplies are running low. They will trade information for safety but will not abandon a patient.

interfaces.note: The doctor has partial understanding of the threat's biology. Enough to be useful, not enough to be safe.
The doctor may have been complicit in early research, or may have stumbled onto records they wish they hadn't read.

entries.claude_text: A junior systems engineer who knows the vessel's infrastructure better than anyone still alive. They are spiraling — talking too fast, jumping at sounds, proposing increasingly desperate plans. They can fix anything mechanical if they can hold their hands steady long enough. They have barricaded themselves in a maintenance bay and will not open the door easily.

interfaces.note: The engineer knows shortcuts and maintenance tunnels that do not appear on standard deck plans.
The engineer may have accidentally triggered or worsened the threat through routine maintenance.
